title: HTTP & Responses
description: Understand the request-response cycle and HTTP status codes.

blocks.content: Every time your browser asks a server for data, it follows the same pattern: your code sends an **HTTP request**, and the server sends back an **HTTP response**. Understanding this cycle is essential for working with `fetch`.

1. Your JavaScript calls `fetch(url)` with the address of the API endpoint.
2. The browser sends an **HTTP request** to the server at that address.
3. The server processes the request and sends back an **HTTP response**.
4. The response contains a **status code** and, usually, a **body** containing JSON data.
5. Your code reads the status code to check whether the request succeeded, then converts the body to a JavaScript object.
Every response includes a **status code** — a three-digit number that tells you whether the request succeeded or failed. Codes are grouped by their first digit:

blocks.caption: HTTP status code ranges
rows: Request was received, understood, and accepted
Further action is needed to complete the request
The request was invalid or cannot be fulfilled
The server failed to process a valid request

blocks.caption: Frequently encountered HTTP status codes
rows: Everything worked — data is in the response body
A new resource was successfully created
The request was malformed or missing required data
Authentication is required or has failed
You are authenticated but don't have permission
The requested resource does not exist
Something went wrong on the server

blocks.content:
The `Response` object returned by `fetch` exposes two useful properties for checking the outcome:

- **`response.status`** — the numeric status code (e.g. `200`, `404`)
- **`response.ok`** — a boolean that is `true` when the status is in the 200–299 range

blocks.caption: Checking response.ok before reading the response body

blocks.title: Important
blocks.content: fetch() only throws an error for network failures (e.g. no internet). A 404 or 500 response does NOT throw — you must check response.ok yourself.

blocks.title: HTTP & Responses Quiz
blocks.description: Test your knowledge of the request-response cycle and status codes.

questions.question: What does a 200 status code mean?
questions.explanation: 200 OK means the request was received, understood, and the response contains the requested data.

questions.question: What does response.ok equal when the server returns a 404?
questions.explanation: response.ok is true only for status codes in the 200–299 range. A 404 returns false.

questions.question: Which status code would you expect when successfully creating a new resource?
questions.explanation: 201 Created is returned when a POST request successfully creates a new resource on the server.

questions.question: When does fetch() throw an error by itself?
options: When the network connection fails entirely
questions.explanation: fetch() only rejects its Promise (throws) on network-level failures such as no internet connection. HTTP error codes like 404 or 500 do not cause fetch() to throw.
